verdict_reasoning: HMGB1 is a canonical non-histone nuclear protein (DNA chaperone / chromatin organiser) with no TM domain, GPI anchor, or outer-leaflet lipidation. Its baseline localisation is nuclear; it has no membrane anchor of its own at any stage of its lifecycle. When released (actively via lysosomal exocytosis or passively during cell death), it enters the extracellular milieu as a fully soluble DAMP that then binds surface receptors (RAGE, TLR4, CD24-Siglec) by reversible non-covalent interaction — it does not remain stably anchored to the outer leaflet after washing. Pre-`no` checklist: (1) No cell-surface-directed therapeutic targets the HMGB1 protein body on the outer leaflet — all clinical/preclinical anti-HMGB1 programs target the soluble secreted pool, not a surface-anchored form. (2) Not a shedding substrate — no TM precursor stage. (3) No wash-resistant covalent or non-covalent tethering to a TM partner documented. (4) No documented PM minority pool with a stable anchor; the transient extracellular pool is fully soluble and in equilibrium. (5) The 5 contextual buckets: cell_state_induced — extracellular HMGB1 is released into the soluble/extracellular pool, not displayed on the outer leaflet of viable cells; tissue_restricted_surface — no tissue-restricted outer-leaflet display with own anchor; lysosomal_exocytosis — secretory lysosome pathway releases HMGB1 into the extracellular milieu as a soluble protein, not as a lysosomal TM protein displayed at PM; dual_localization — no documented PM pool with stable anchor; stable_surface_attachment — binding to RAGE/TLRs is reversible non-covalent, not wash-resistant. Verdict: no / secreted_only.
